key_insight: Bulk dealloying can proceed well below the classical parting limit in molten salt because diffusion-induced recrystallization creates dense grain-boundary pathways that enable infiltration and selective dissolution.
engineering_relevance: This finding helps predict and control corrosion and porous-structure formation in high-temperature electrochemical systems by showing grain-boundary evolution can override composition-based dealloying limits.
method: Mapped grain-boundary/void architecture to infer the dealloying pathway
evidence_signal: The abstract reports direct 3D mapping of the dealloyed regions and grain-boundary-void network, linking the observed morphology to a grain-boundary-assisted bulk dealloying mechanism.
watch_item: The mechanism is demonstrated for molten-salt environments, so transferability to other chemistries, alloys, or temperature regimes may require case-specific validation.
paper_title: Anomalous bulk dealloying below the parting limit
